name: Lake Michigan
description_short: Lake Michigan is a geographical feature, not a financial services company. This is a Britannica encyclopedia entry about one of the five Great Lakes.
description_long: Lake Michigan is the third-largest of the five Great Lakes of North America and the only Great Lake lying wholly within the United States. According to the Britannica encyclopedia entry provided, it is a natural freshwater lake bordered by Michigan, Wisconsin, Illinois, and Indiana. The website content is an educational reference article describing the lake's physical characteristics, geography, and economic importance to the region—not a consumer finance company offering any financial products or services. The entry details the lake's dimensions (321 miles long, 118 miles wide at maximum), depth (923 feet maximum), and surface area (22,300 square miles). It describes the lake's role in regional commerce, including its use for transporting iron ore, coal, limestone, and grain through ports in Chicago, Gary, Milwaukee, and other cities. The article also discusses the lake's geography, including sand dunes, bluffs, and islands, as well as its historical significance in regional development and the reversal of the Chicago River in 1900. There is no company information, financial products, lending terms, interest rates, or consumer finance services described in this content.
